Article Eater antecedent: High Fractal Dimension (2D)
Article Eater antecedent: High Seating Density
Article Eater antecedent: High Spatial Frequency Content
Article Eater antecedent: High Texture Complexity
Article Eater antecedent: Large Enclosure Volume
Article Eater antecedent: Large Window Area
Article Eater antecedent: Low Edge Density
Article Eater antecedent: Low Spatial Frequency Content
Article Eater antecedent: Low Texture Complexity
Article Eater antecedent: Poor Visibility / Dark Corners
Article Eater antecedent: Soft Acoustic Treatments
Article Eater antecedent: Strong Central Landmark
Article Eater antecedent: Surveillance / Cameras Visible
Article Eater antecedent: Text/Information Density High
Article Eater antecedent: View to Outdoors
Article Eater antecedent: Visible HVAC Vents
Article Eater antecedent: Warm Color Palette
Article Eater antecedent: Work Surfaces Present
Morning light most impactful for entrainment
Color palette hue/saturation/lightness harmony (beyond CCT); red/blue dominance (Mehta & Zhu, 2009).
Warm vs cool white appearance (CCT) and perceived coziness/activation (Cajochen et al., 2005; Chellappa et al., 2011).
Brightness level at eye (vertical illuminance) vs task plane; perceived ‘dimness’ vs ‘glare’ zones.
Brightness gradients and brightness contrast ratios (accent lighting vs uniform lighting).
Daylight presence, sky view, and view-to-outside content (nature/urban).
Glare source count (direct & reflected) and discomfort glare probability proxies.
Flicker/temporal modulation risk cues (LED drivers; visible strobing in videos).
Color rendering / saturation rendering (skin-tone naturalness) cues.
Shadow softness/hardness (diffuse vs directional) as comfort/threat cues.
Lighting control cues: dimmers, blinds, task lights (autonomy).
Evening-like ambience cues vs daytime-like ambience cues (circadian appropriateness).
Visual privacy via lighting: backlit silhouettes vs evenly lit faces (social comfort).
High contrast ‘spotlight’ social exposure cues (stage-like lighting).
Presence of natural light patterns (dappled light, sun patches) (thermal/affective associations).
Lighting ‘temperature mismatch’ (e.g., very cool lighting in ‘relax’ contexts) as appraisal mismatch.
Ceiling height and openness (processing style; freedom/constraint primes) (Meyers-Levy & Zhu, 2007).
Room scale cues (human scale vs monumental) and awe/insignificance appraisal.
Enclosure ratio: wall-to-window balance; perceived refuge vs exposure.
Prospect–refuge: ability to see without being seen; corner refuge availability.
Exit visibility and path-to-exit complexity (safety appraisal).
Blind corners vs transparent partitions; concealment opportunities.
Distance to others (proxemic space) and crowding stress risk (Evans & Wener, 2007).
Wayfinding legibility: straight sightlines, landmarks, signage, decision-point density.
Spatial complexity: branching corridors vs simple loops (cognitive load).
Perceived territoriality: defined zones vs ambiguous shared space.
Barrier permeability: half-walls vs full walls; social exposure control.
Visual access to outdoors/nature as ‘escape’ affordance.
Seating prospect: facing entrance vs back to entrance (threat monitoring).
Personal safety affordances: lighting in corners, absence of hiding places.
Curvature prevalence vs sharp angles (threat/comfort bias) (Bar & Neta, 2006).
Fractal/self-similar edge statistics (nature-like ranges) (Hägerhäll et al., 2004).
Clutter/disorder: object density, occlusion, surface coverage (stress/control).
Color diversity and saturation; visual ‘loudness’ as arousal potential.
Texture density (busy patterns) vs minimalism (calmness in some contexts).
Artwork density and semantic content (threatening vs soothing imagery).
Naturalness of materials (wood grain) vs synthetic visual noise.
Visual complexity gradients (quiet corners vs busy hubs).
Signage density (informational load; can reduce uncertainty or increase clutter).
Visual affordances for organization (storage visibility) reducing disorder.
Orderliness of lines: alignment, grid structure, repetition.
Perceptual ‘mystery’ cues (partial occlusion inviting exploration) (ART literature).
Visible vegetation quantity, proximity, and distribution (Kaplan, 1995).
Window view content: greenery, sky, water vs built-up (Ulrich, 1984).
Natural materials: wood/stone; biomorphic forms; nature images.
Prospect to distant views (horizon) vs close enclosure.
Natural ventilation cues (breeze, operable windows) as ‘nature contact’.
Natural sound sources (water fountain) as restorative cue.
Seasonal cues (flowers, fall colors) and mood associations.
Animal presence cues (fish tanks) and soothing engagement.
Sociopetal seating (faces toward) vs sociofugal (faces away) arrangements.
Density/crowding proxies: chairs per area, people present, queueing lines (Evans & Wener, 2007).
Surveillance cues: cameras, exposed workstations, panoramic visibility (social evaluative stress).
Privacy cues: partitions, doors, curtains; acoustic barriers.
Territoriality markers: name plates, personal objects, assigned desks.
Norm cues: formal vs informal (dress code signs, etiquette cues) affecting anxiety.
Shared-resource competition cues (few outlets, few seats) affecting stress.
Social mixing affordances (common tables) vs isolation affordances (booths).
Conflict/overhearing risk cues (tight spacing) affecting willingness to speak.
User controls visible and accessible: dimmers, blinds, thermostats (Lee & Brand, 2010).
Furniture adjustability: movable chairs/tables, reconfigurable partitions.
Choice richness: multiple zones (quiet/social/active) enabling self-selection.
Personalization affordances: pinboards, shelves, plant allowance.
Policy cues restricting control (signs forbidding moving furniture).
Control over privacy: doors, curtains, screens.
Control over sound: headsets, phone booths, white noise.
Control over thermal: fans, heaters, seat locations relative to windows.
Control over lighting: task lamps, individual switches.
Information control: clear wayfinding reduces uncertainty.
